properties.tree_location: backyard on the left near neighbor's privacy fence
properties.tree_description:
Damaged oak treeReason for Removal:Dying treeComments:The subject tree is a mature oak (~25" DBH) with significant structural defects. The main stem contains embedded metal (metal post and fencing wire), which compromises wood fiber integrity, creates potential internal decay pathways, and limits feasible mitigation or safe pruning. The tree exhibits a lean toward the residence, placing a high value target within the fall zone. It also has a documented history of recurrent limb drop, indicating declining structural reliability. Due to trunk defects, target proximity, and ongoing limb failure, the tree presents an unacceptable risk that cannot be adequately mitigated through pruning or monitoring. Removal is recommended.

Very large white oak with fungus. Reason for Removal:Dying treeComments:Microscopic examination of the submitted sample revealed the oak bracket fungus, Pseudoinonotus dryadeus. This
decay fungus is found near the base of oak and true fir trees. This fungus is closely related to and was previously
classified within Inonotus, many of which can cause extensive decay of the trunk, root flare, and buttress
roots.?This is a white rot which may reduce structural integrity by degrading lignin and/or cellulose. This fungus
may grow alone or in clusters and is generally present between summer and fall. The fruiting bodies of this fungus
have an amorphous shape, a light yellow/buff color of the surface, and golden droplets along the surface.

Due to poor limb structure and proximity to the home, the tree presents a hazardous condition with risk of major limb failure and property damage.
